html:
Seoho Electric is best understood as a tiny, software-heavy Asian port automation supplier, not a generic shipbuilding cycle stock. It designs crane drive and control systems, inverters, converters, anti-sway, positioning, monitoring, and remote/automated operation systems for port and shipyard cranes. That matters because the demand driver is not simply “more ships.” It is ports replacing labor-intensive, unsafe, manually operated terminals with automated electric yards.

The market’s lazy label is that Seoho had a one-time 2025 project profit spike. The better read is that 2025 proved operating leverage in a scarce niche. Revenue rose to KRW 116.6 billion, operating income to KRW 25.3 billion, and net income to KRW 20.7 billion, while free cash flow reached KRW 36.1 billion (financials). Q1 2026 looked optically down year over year because Q1 2025 was abnormally strong, but it was still very profitable: KRW 25.9 billion revenue and KRW 8.04 billion operating profit, about a 31% operating margin.

The variant perception is that Seoho is a mission-critical controls integrator in a market with few credible references. LS Securities framed the competitive set as ABB in the Americas, Siemens in Europe, and Seoho in Asia, with project references acting as the barrier (LS report). The company’s CEO says Seoho has decades of retrofit experience in Singapore, supplied 130 automated RMGs there in 2015, and focuses on system integration rather than low-margin parts (Worldfolio). That is exactly the niche that should benefit as Tuas, Busan, Gwangyang, Incheon, Jinhae, and other Asian terminals automate.

The macro backdrop is unusually supportive. PSA says Tuas is already the world’s largest fully automated container terminal and is designed for 65 million TEU capacity when completed in the 2040s (PSA). Busan’s automated DGT terminal is now running unmanned crane and AGV flows, with automation said to lift logistics efficiency by up to 30%, and DGT plans to expand to 219 unmanned units (ChosunBiz). Seoho’s September 2025 KRW 13.4 billion HD Hyundai Samho contract for Dongwon Global Terminal Busan DTQC equipment runs into 2029, so the backlog is not just a 2025 story (contract filing summary).

The balance sheet is good, but the thesis should not be reduced to “net cash.” At year-end 2025, StockAnalysis showed KRW 32.2 billion cash and essentially no debt. Then Seoho sold 514,485 treasury shares for KRW 26.3 billion to fund imported raw materials and working capital (treasury sale). After the final KRW 2,000 dividend, current net cash is roughly KRW 45-50 billion. That is useful downside protection, but the real attraction is that the operating business is capable of KRW 25-35 billion annual EBIT in a good project mix year with very little balance-sheet leverage.

Capital return is also better than the typical Korean small-cap discount. Seoho paid KRW 2,000 interim dividends for both June and September 2025 and another KRW 2,000 final dividend for FY2025, totaling KRW 6,000 per share, and its March 2026 value-up plan explicitly targets sustainable dividends, stronger R&D, production efficiency, and new market development (dividend, value-up plan). The treasury sale is not as clean as a buyback cancellation, but selling treasury stock near the highs to fund a larger revenue base is more tolerable than issuing cheap equity.

At the latest K5 delayed quote I found today of KRW 47,250, Seoho’s market cap is roughly KRW 243 billion using 5.15 million issued shares (K5 quote). That is about 11-12x conservative 2026 earnings power, or roughly 7x EV/EBIT if the company sustains KRW 28-30 billion EBIT and pro forma net cash is credited. This is no longer the absurdly cheap KRW 20,000 stock. But it is still cheap if the right label is “high-ROIC port automation integrator with recurring retrofit optionality,” not “lumpy Korean electrical contractor.”

The main risks are real. Project accounting is lumpy, and backlog recognition can make any quarter misleading. Customer concentration is high, with exposure to ZPMC, HD Hyundai Samho, HJ Heavy, Busan Port projects, and major terminal operators. A pause in Asian port capex after the current wave would compress 2027-2028 earnings. The January treasury share sale increased tradable supply and showed working-capital needs are rising with revenue. Competition from ABB, Siemens, Chinese crane OEMs, and in-house terminal technology is always a risk. The stock is also thin and retail-driven, with very low institutional ownership, so drawdowns can be abrupt.

My base case is that 2026-2027 revenue settles around KRW 105-125 billion, operating income runs KRW 28-32 billion, dividends remain meaningful, and the market gives the business a 10x EBIT multiple plus cash. That supports about KRW 68,000 per share.

Bear case: KRW 30,000, down 37%. Revenue normalizes faster than expected, new orders disappoint, EBIT falls toward KRW 18 billion, and the market treats 2025 as peak. Cash and dividends limit permanent impairment, but the multiple contracts.

Base case: KRW 68,000, up 44%. Seoho converts existing Busan and Singapore work, wins enough replacement automation orders, sustains roughly KRW 30 billion EBIT, and retains a high dividend yield.

Bull case: KRW 95,000, up 101%. Port automation becomes the dominant narrative, Seoho wins additional Tuas, Busan, Gwangyang, Jinhae, or overseas retrofit work, EBIT reaches KRW 38 billion, and the market rerates the company as a scarce smart-port automation compounder rather than a project-cycle contractor.
